topic: Hot Desert Systems & Landscapes / Coastal Systems & Landscapes / Glacial Systems & Landscapes
subject: geography

items.q: What does a 'large scale' map mean?
items.correct: A large-scale map covers a small area in high detail (e.g. 1:25,000 OS map of one town); a small-scale map covers a large area in less detail (e.g. 1:1,000,000 world atlas).
items.misconception: A large-scale map shows a large area.
items.random_wrong: Large scale means the map is physically big.
items.why: This is counter-intuitive but mathematically logical: 1/25,000 is a larger fraction than 1/1,000,000. Students reverse the terminology and consistently mark themselves down on map skill questions.

items.q: What is the enhanced greenhouse effect?
items.correct: The strengthening of the natural greenhouse effect by human activities (mainly burning fossil fuels) which add CO2 and other gases to the atmosphere.
items.misconception: The greenhouse effect is entirely man-made and harmful.
items.random_wrong: It is the hole in the ozone layer letting in more sun.
items.why: The natural greenhouse effect is essential for life — without it Earth would be -18 degrees C. The problem is enhancement. Students also confuse it with the (separate) ozone hole.

items.q: Why is the Demographic Transition Model criticised for not applying to all countries?
items.correct: It was based on European industrialisation. LICs today face different conditions: imported medicine without industrialisation, cultural factors, gender inequality and globalised economies — not all follow the same path.
items.misconception: All countries follow the five stages of the DTM in order.
items.random_wrong: The DTM only applies to countries with more than 50 million people.
items.why: Many LICs experienced rapid death rate falls without industrialisation, breaking the model's logic. Some HICs are entering a Stage 5 it didn't predict — the DTM is a starting point, not a universal law.

items.q: Which glacier type erodes most actively?
items.why: It's Warm-based. “Cold-based” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Sheep rock. “Ice mountain” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Antarctic contribution to sea level rise in mm/year?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 mm/year. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
